questions.question: Which of the following is an indication for BVM ventilation?
options: A. Normal rate and depth of breathing
B. Inadequate tidal volume
C. Mild anxiety
D. A patient who is talking in full sentences
questions.rationale: Inadequate tidal volume is a core BVM indication, along with apnea, bradypnea, cyanosis, and altered mental status with poor respiratory effort.

questions.question: A patient is unresponsive with no gag reflex. Which airway adjunct is appropriate?
options: A. NPA
B. OPA
C. Neither is appropriate
D. Only an advanced airway
questions.rationale: An OPA is used in unresponsive patients without a gag reflex.

questions.question: A semi-conscious patient with an intact gag reflex needs an airway adjunct. Which is preferred?
options: A. OPA
B. NPA
C. No adjunct can be used
D. Endotracheal tube only
questions.rationale: The NPA is generally better tolerated in patients with an intact gag reflex or who are semi-conscious.

questions.question: In which situation is the NPA contraindicated?
options: A. Simple nosebleed
B. Suspected basilar skull fracture or significant mid-face trauma
C. Intact gag reflex
D. Semi-consciousness
questions.rationale: Suspected basilar skull fracture or significant mid-face trauma is a contraindication to the NPA due to the risk of intracranial tube placement.

questions.question: A patient having a seizure vomits during the postictal period. What is the immediate airway action?
options: A. Insert an OPA immediately regardless of gag reflex
B. Turn the patient onto their side to allow drainage, then suction as needed
C. Sit the patient fully upright
D. Withhold any airway intervention until fully alert
questions.rationale: Positioning on the side allows vomit to drain out rather than pool or be aspirated; suction addresses what positioning alone cannot clear.

questions.question: What does stridor indicate?
options: A. Lower airway bronchospasm
B. Upper airway obstruction
C. Normal breath sounds
D. Pleural effusion
questions.rationale: Stridor is a high-pitched sound indicating upper airway (proximal) obstruction, distinct from lower-airway wheezing, and is potentially life-threatening.

questions.question: A patient is breathing 14 times per minute but with minimal visible chest rise. What should you consider?
options: A. Breathing is adequate since the rate is normal
B. Tidal volume may be inadequate despite a normal rate, warranting assisted ventilation
C. No further assessment is needed
D. This always indicates cardiac arrest
questions.rationale: Rate alone does not confirm adequate ventilation; poor tidal volume at a normal rate can still require BVM support.

questions.question: Why does altered mental status increase airway management priority across many different diagnoses?
options: A. It doesn't — airway risk depends only on the specific diagnosis
B. Altered LOC removes protective airway reflexes (cough, swallow, tongue tone), regardless of the underlying cause
C. Only trauma patients with altered LOC have airway risk
D. Altered mental status has no bearing on airway management
questions.rationale: Loss of protective reflexes is a common risk factor across overdose, stroke, sepsis, and other causes of altered LOC — the airway priority is consistent even when the diagnosis differs.

questions.question: A patient bites down and gags on an inserted OPA. What should you do?
options: A. Force it further in
B. Remove it — a gag reflex means the OPA is not tolerated
C. Leave it in place and proceed
D. Switch to a larger OPA
questions.rationale: An OPA is only for patients without a gag reflex; if gagging occurs, the OPA should be removed to avoid triggering vomiting or aspiration.

questions.question: Which finding should make you treat an airway complaint with the highest urgency?
options: A. Mild wheeze on exhalation
B. New-onset stridor
C. A slightly elevated respiratory rate
D. A cough with clear sputum
questions.rationale: New stridor signals proximal airway obstruction that can progress to complete obstruction rapidly, requiring urgent escalation and preparation for advanced airway support.

questions.question: A fall victim with suspected basilar skull fracture needs an airway adjunct and has an intact gag reflex. What should you use?
options: A. NPA, since gag reflex is present
B. OPA is not appropriate (gag present) and NPA is contraindicated (skull fracture); manage the airway by positioning/suction and consider other adjuncts per protocol
C. Force an NPA regardless of the fracture risk
D. No airway management is needed
questions.rationale: With an intact gag reflex, OPA is inappropriate; with suspected basilar skull fracture, NPA is contraindicated due to intracranial placement risk — positioning, suction, and other protocol-appropriate measures apply instead.

questions.question: Cyanosis in a breathing patient is significant because it suggests:
options: A. Adequate oxygenation
B. Inadequate oxygenation, an indication to consider assisted ventilation
C. A normal finding in all patients
D. A skin allergy
questions.rationale: Cyanosis reflects inadequate oxygenation and is one of the listed indications for BVM ventilation.

questions.question: Which pairing is correct regarding airway adjunct selection?
options: A. Gag reflex present → OPA
B. Gag reflex absent (unresponsive) → OPA; gag reflex present/semi-conscious → NPA
C. Both adjuncts are interchangeable regardless of gag reflex
D. Neither adjunct depends on gag reflex status
questions.rationale: OPA use requires absence of a gag reflex; NPA is chosen when a gag reflex is present or the patient is semi-conscious, as it is generally better tolerated.

questions.question: Why is stridor described as a 'red flag' finding rather than a routine respiratory sound?
options: A. Because it is always benign
B. Because it indicates a proximal airway problem that can rapidly progress to complete obstruction
C. Because it only occurs in healthy patients
D. Because it requires no further action
questions.rationale: The proximal location of the obstruction causing stridor means airway compromise can worsen quickly, demanding urgent recognition and action.
